quiz_questions.question_text: Which kinematic equation relates velocity, initial velocity, acceleration, and time?

options.text: s = ut + ½at²
v = u + at
v² = u² + 2as
s = ½(u + v)t

quiz_questions.explanation: The first equation of motion v = u + at directly links final velocity (v) to initial velocity (u), acceleration (a), and time (t).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which kinematic equation is used to find displacement when you know initial velocity, time, and acceleration?

options.text: v² = u² + 2as
a = Δv/Δt
v = u + at
s = ut + ½at²

quiz_questions.explanation: s = ut + ½at² gives displacement as a function of initial velocity, acceleration, and time. It is derived by integrating v = u + at with respect to time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which kinematic equation relates final velocity, initial velocity, acceleration, and displacement without time?

options.text: v² = u² + 2as
s = ut + ½at²
v = u + at
s = vt − ½at²

quiz_questions.explanation: v² = u² + 2as eliminates time and directly connects velocity and displacement. It is obtained by combining v = u + at with s = ½(u + v)t.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object starts from rest and accelerates at 4 m/s² for 3 seconds. What is its final velocity?

options.text: 7 m/s
36 m/s
12 m/s
4 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: Using v = u + at with u = 0: v = 0 + 4(3) = 12 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object starts from rest and accelerates at 2 m/s² for 5 seconds. What displacement does it cover?

options.text: 5 m

quiz_questions.explanation: Using s = ut + ½at² with u = 0: s = 0 + ½(2)(25) = 25 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car traveling at 20 m/s brakes and stops in 4 seconds. What is the acceleration?

options.text: −20 m/s²
−5 m/s²
5 m/s²
−4 m/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: Using v = u + at: 0 = 20 + a(4) → a = −20/4 = −5 m/s². The negative sign indicates deceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the variable 'u' represent in the kinematic equations?

options.text: Initial velocity
Final velocity
Displacement
Acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: In the standard kinematic equations (v = u + at, s = ut + ½at², v² = u² + 2as), u represents the initial velocity at time t = 0.

quiz_questions.question_text: The kinematic equations apply only when which condition is met?

options.text: There is no friction
Velocity is constant
The object moves in a circle
Acceleration is constant

quiz_questions.explanation: The standard kinematic equations (SUVAT equations) are derived assuming constant (uniform) acceleration. They do not apply when acceleration changes with time.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object has initial velocity 10 m/s and accelerates at 3 m/s² for 4 seconds. What is the displacement?

options.text: 52 m — using the formula s = u + at squared gives s = 10 + 3 times 16 = 10 + 48 = 52 m of total displacement traveled
124 m — multiplying all three values together gives s = u times a times t = 10 times 3 times 4 = 120 plus 4 = 124 m displacement
40 m — using only the first term of the equation gives s = ut = 10 times 4 = 40 m, ignoring the acceleration contribution

quiz_questions.question_text: How many standard kinematic equations are there for constant acceleration in one dimension?

options.text: Two — there are only two fundamental kinematic equations, and any others are simply restatements using different variable arrangements
Ten — there are ten independent kinematic equations because each combination of the five variables produces a unique relationship
One — there is only one master kinematic equation from which all motion relationships can be directly read without rearrangement
Four (sometimes listed as three main equations plus one auxiliary)

quiz_questions.explanation: There are typically four kinematic equations: v = u + at, s = ut + ½at², v² = u² + 2as, and s = ½(u + v)t. Any one can be derived from the other three.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the equations of motion?

options.text: Equations that describe the forces acting on an object, including gravity, friction, and normal force, to predict the net force and resulting motion
Any equation used in any branch of physics, including thermodynamics, electromagnetism, optics, and quantum mechanics, not just kinematics
Formulas that only apply to objects in free fall under gravity, and cannot be used for objects that are launched, braking, or moving horizontally
A set of mathematical relationships that connect displacement, initial velocity, final velocity, acceleration, and time for an object under constant acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: The equations of motion (kinematic equations) relate five variables (s, u, v, a, t) for uniformly accelerated motion. Each equation omits one variable.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes constant (uniform) acceleration?

options.text: Acceleration that remains the same in both magnitude and direction throughout the entire motion
Acceleration that is always equal to gravity
Acceleration that increases steadily over time
Any acceleration in a straight line

quiz_questions.explanation: Constant acceleration means the rate of change of velocity does not vary with time — the velocity increases (or decreases) by equal amounts in equal time intervals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes initial velocity?

options.text: The velocity when acceleration becomes zero
The velocity of an object at the start of the time interval being analyzed, often denoted u or v₀
The maximum velocity reached
The velocity at the exact midpoint of a journey

quiz_questions.explanation: Initial velocity (u or v₀) is the velocity at time t = 0 for the interval under consideration. It serves as a starting condition for the kinematic equations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes final velocity?

options.text: The velocity of an object at the end of the time interval being analyzed, denoted v
The velocity just before the object stops permanently
The velocity at the midpoint of the journey
The fastest speed achieved during motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Final velocity (v) is the velocity at the end of the chosen time interval. In v = u + at, it represents the velocity after time t has elapsed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes displacement in the context of kinematic equations?

options.text: The total path length traveled by an object
The straight-line distance and direction from the initial position to the final position of an object, denoted s
The change in acceleration over time
The speed multiplied by acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement (s) is a vector quantity representing the change in position. In uniform acceleration, s = ut + ½at² or s = ½(u + v)t.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a SUVAT equation?

options.text: A special equation developed specifically for circular motion, relating centripetal acceleration to the radius and angular velocity of a revolving object
An acronym for a particular type of force equation that connects applied force, mass, friction coefficient, and the resulting net acceleration
One of the kinematic equations named after the five variables they relate: displacement (s), initial velocity (u), final velocity (v), acceleration (a), and time (t)
Equations used exclusively in the United Kingdom physics curriculum and not recognized or applicable in other international education systems

quiz_questions.explanation: SUVAT stands for the five kinematic variables. Each SUVAT equation relates four of these five variables, making it possible to solve for any unknown given three knowns.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes deceleration?

options.text: Acceleration that always points in the downward direction, since deceleration can only occur when an object is moving vertically against gravity
Any negative number that appears in a physics equation, regardless of whether it represents velocity, displacement, force, or some other quantity
Acceleration that acts opposite to the direction of motion, causing the object to slow down — represented by a negative value of a when velocity is positive
The complete absence of acceleration, meaning the object maintains a perfectly constant velocity with no change in speed or direction at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Deceleration is not a separate physical quantity — it is acceleration directed opposite to motion. When an object with positive velocity has negative acceleration, it slows down (decelerates).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes free fall in the context of kinematic equations?

options.text: Motion in which the object has zero velocity at all times, remaining perfectly stationary at a fixed location throughout the entire observation period
Falling at a speed that is always exactly equal to the gravitational constant g, never accelerating beyond or decelerating below that fixed speed value
Motion under the sole influence of gravity with constant acceleration g ≈ 9.8 m/s² directed downward, with no air resistance
Any motion in which an object moves in a downward direction, regardless of whether engines, air resistance, or other forces are also acting on it

quiz_questions.explanation: Free fall is a special case of constant acceleration where the only influence is gravity. In this case a = g ≈ 9.8 m/s² downward. The kinematic equations apply directly with a = ±g.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the time of flight?

options.text: The time an object spends at its highest point in the trajectory, where velocity momentarily equals zero before the object begins falling back down
The total time an object remains in motion from launch to landing, calculable from the kinematic equations by setting final displacement to zero
The time required for an object to reach its maximum speed during the acceleration phase, after which the object coasts at constant velocity
The time required for one complete revolution of an object moving in a circular path, also known as the orbital period of the circular motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Time of flight is the total duration of a projectile's or thrown object's trip. For vertical motion returning to the same height: T = 2u/g.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes uniform motion?

options.text: Motion that is uniform in color and shape
Any motion described by Newton's laws
Motion at constant velocity (zero acceleration), where the kinematic equations simplify to s = vt
Motion in a perfect circle

quiz_questions.explanation: Uniform motion means a = 0. The kinematic equations reduce to v = u (velocity doesn't change) and s = ut (displacement increases linearly with time).

quiz_questions.question_text: A sprinter starts from rest and accelerates at 3 m/s² for 4 seconds. How far does she run in that time?

options.text: 48 m — using s = at squared without the one-half factor gives s = 3 times 16 = 48 m, which represents the distance at full acceleration rate
12 m — using s = at gives s = 3 times 4 = 12 m, since distance equals the product of acceleration and time in uniformly accelerated motion
6 m — using s = half times a times t gives s = 0.5 times 3 times 4 = 6 m, forgetting to square the time value in the displacement formula

quiz_questions.question_text: A car is traveling at 25 m/s and brakes uniformly to a stop over a distance of 62.5 m. What is the deceleration?

options.text: −25 m/s² — using a = v minus u divided by s gives a = (0 minus 25) divided by 1 = −25 m/s², confusing distance with time in the formula
−10 m/s² — using a = u divided by s gives a = 25 divided by 2.5 = 10 m/s² with a negative sign added, yielding −10 m/s² deceleration
−2.5 m/s² — using a = u divided by 2s gives a = 25 divided by 125 = 0.2 then inverted to 2.5 m/s² with a negative sign for braking

quiz_questions.explanation: v² = u² + 2as → 0 = 625 + 125a → a = −625/125 = −5 m/s². The negative sign correctly indicates deceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A stone is thrown vertically upward at 15 m/s. Using g = 10 m/s², how long does it take to reach its highest point?

options.text: 1.5 s — at the top v = 0, so 0 = 15 − 10t → t = 1.5 s
0.75 s — using t = u divided by 2g gives t = 15 divided by 20 = 0.75 s, which accounts for both the upward and downward phases
3.0 s — this is the total flight time (up and back down), obtained by doubling the time to the highest point using t = 2u divided by g
15 s — using t = u divided by 1 gives t = 15 s, mistakenly treating the initial velocity value as the time to reach the highest point

quiz_questions.explanation: At the highest point, v = 0. Using v = u + at: 0 = 15 + (−10)t → t = 15/10 = 1.5 s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A motorcycle accelerates from 10 m/s to 30 m/s over a distance of 200 m. What is the acceleration?

options.text: 0.1 m/s² — using a = (v minus u) divided by s gives a = 20 divided by 200 = 0.1 m/s², confusing displacement with time in the calculation
1 m/s² — using a = (v minus u) divided by 2s gives a = 20 divided by 400 = 0.05, then rounding up to approximately 1 m/s² acceleration
4 m/s² — using a = (v squared minus u squared) divided by s gives a = 800 divided by 200 = 4 m/s², forgetting the factor of 2 in the formula

quiz_questions.question_text: Two students solve the same problem. Student A uses v = u + at and Student B uses v² = u² + 2as. Both get the same final velocity. Is this expected?

options.text: No — different kinematic equations always give different numerical answers because each equation models a different aspect of the physical situation
Only if the initial velocity is exactly zero, because the kinematic equations are only consistent with each other when the object starts from rest
Only if the acceleration is exactly zero, since the equations diverge and give conflicting results whenever there is any nonzero acceleration present
Yes — all kinematic equations are derived from the same assumptions and are mathematically equivalent; they must give consistent answers for the same physical situation

quiz_questions.explanation: All SUVAT equations are derived from the same two fundamental relationships (v = u + at and s = ½(u + v)t) under constant acceleration. They are algebraically consistent and always give the same results.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball rolling on a flat surface at 12 m/s decelerates at 2 m/s² due to friction. When the ball stops, a student calculates the distance as s = v² − u² / 2a = (0 − 144) / (2 × −2) = 36 m. Is this correct?

options.text: No — the answer should be negative
No — you cannot use this equation for deceleration
Yes — using v² = u² + 2as rearranged: s = (v² − u²)/(2a) = (0 − 144)/(−4) = 36 m
No — the formula should give 72 m

quiz_questions.explanation: s = (v² − u²)/(2a) = (0 − 144)/(2 × −2) = (−144)/(−4) = 36 m. The two negatives cancel, giving a positive displacement as expected for forward motion while braking.

quiz_questions.question_text: A rocket accelerates at 15 m/s² from rest for 10 seconds, then its engines cut out and it decelerates at 10 m/s² until it stops. What is the total distance traveled?

options.text: 1500 m — using only the first phase: s = half times 15 times 100 = 750 m then doubling for the deceleration phase gives a total of 1500 m distance
2250 m — using s = v times t for both phases: 150 times 10 = 1500 m plus 150 times 15 divided by 2 = 1125, total is roughly 2250 m distance
750 m — calculating only the acceleration phase distance: s = half times 15 times 100 = 750 m and ignoring the coasting and deceleration phases

quiz_questions.question_text: An elevator starts from rest, accelerates upward at 1.5 m/s² for 4 s, moves at constant velocity for 8 s, then decelerates at 1.5 m/s² for 4 s. What is the total distance?

options.text: 72 m — Phase 1: s = half times 1.5 times 16 = 12 m; Phase 2: s = 6 times 8 = 48 m; Phase 3: s = 12 m; Total = 12 + 48 + 12 = 72 m traveled
36 m — using only the constant velocity phase and halving it: s = 6 times 8 divided by 2 = 24 m, plus 12 m from the other phases = 36 m total
48 m — counting only the constant velocity middle phase: s = 6 times 8 = 48 m and treating the acceleration and deceleration as contributing zero

quiz_questions.question_text: A police car starts from rest and accelerates at 4 m/s². A speeding car passes it at a constant 20 m/s. How long until the police car catches the speeding car?

options.text: 20 s — dividing the speeder's velocity by the police acceleration gives t = v divided by a = 20 divided by 4 = 5, then doubling yields 20 seconds
4 s — dividing the police acceleration by the speeder's velocity gives t = a divided by v = 4 divided by 20 = 0.2, then inverting yields about 4 s
5 s — using the average velocity method: the police car's average speed equals half the speeder's speed at t = 5 s, so they meet at t = 5 seconds
10 s — set distances equal: ½(4)t² = 20t → 2t² = 20t → t = 10 s (ignoring t = 0)

quiz_questions.explanation: Police: s₁ = ½(4)t² = 2t². Speeder: s₂ = 20t. Setting equal: 2t² = 20t → t(2t − 20) = 0 → t = 10 s (t = 0 is the starting point).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student writes s = ut + ½at² and substitutes u = 0, a = 9.8, t = 3 to calculate how far an object falls from rest in 3 seconds. She gets 44.1 m. Her friend says the answer is wrong because she didn't include a negative sign. Who is correct?

options.text: The student is always correct — distance can never be negative under any circumstances, so using a positive value of g is the only valid approach in physics
The friend is always correct — the acceleration due to gravity must always carry a negative sign regardless of coordinate system or direction conventions
Both can be correct depending on the coordinate system — if downward is positive, s = +44.1 m is the distance fallen; if upward is positive, s = −44.1 m but the distance is still 44.1 m
Neither student is correct — the entire calculation itself contains a mathematical error because the formula s = ut + half at squared does not apply here

quiz_questions.explanation: The sign of g depends on the chosen coordinate system. With downward positive, a = +9.8 m/s² and s = +44.1 m. With upward positive, a = −9.8 m/s² and s = −44.1 m. Both give 44.1 m fallen.
